title: Ways to think about token pricing — Benedict Evans
gist: Today's AI token pricing power is an artifact of a temporary supply crunch driven by one narrow use case (coding), not a durable moat; on every visible dynamic — competition, cost curves, value capture — frontier models are more likely to end up as low-margin commodity infrastructure than to retain pricing power, unless something not yet visible (a network effect, execution advantage, or regulatory intervention) changes that path.

themes.theme: The supply crunch is transitory, not structural

insights.claim: Today's AI token pricing power is best explained by a temporary supply crunch rather than durable competitive advantage, and the crunch is expected to unwind as capacity catches up.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Investors should treat current inference pricing and margins as a cyclical peak, not a steady-state moat, when valuing model labs.
insights.quote: There are only two things you can say with certainty about token prices: we’re in a supply crunch, and this is unstable.

insights.claim: The first-half-2026 AI capacity crunch was driven almost entirely by sudden product-market fit in one narrow use case, software development, and today's infrastructure could not support a mass-consumer AI use case with hundreds of millions of daily active users at any price.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: A single new consumer use case reaching scale could re-trigger an even more severe capacity crunch than the current one, since existing buildout is calibrated to a narrow demand base.
insights.quote: the crunch in the first half of this year has been driven by sudden product-market fit in really just one use case, software development, and that’s actually a pretty small field

insights.claim: More than a trillion dollars of data center capex, plus additional semiconductor capex, is already in the pipeline, and inference efficiency is improving rapidly, both of which point toward easing supply constraints over the next few years.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Capacity and efficiency gains are a structural headwind to today's inference pricing; investors should expect margin compression as this capex lands, not immediate cliff timing.
insights.quote: a trillion dollars or more of data centre capex is coming down the pipe (and plenty more semiconductor capex behind that), inference efficiency continues to improve very quickly

themes.theme: Frontier model unit economics don't yet prove durable profitability

insights.claim: Inference is reported to run at 40-50% gross margins, but this figure includes only server depreciation and excludes the cost of training the next model, which currently far exceeds revenue.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Headline inference margins overstate model-lab profitability; investors should model total economics (training amortization included) rather than inference gross margin alone.
insights.quote: it’s been pretty widely reported that inference today has 40-50% gross margins: this includes deprecation of the associated server costs (or the cost of renting them)... this doesn’t include the cost of training the next model a couple of times a year, which is currently far larger than revenue

insights.claim: Because inference is a marginal cost and training is a fixed cost, sufficiently high revenue could in principle push a model lab to profitability, but nobody currently knows how training costs will trend, making this an open rather than settled question.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Profitability timing for frontier labs hinges on an unknown variable (future training cost trajectory); treat lab profitability forecasts with skepticism until that trend is visible.
insights.quote: inference is a marginal cost and training is a fixed cost, so with high enough revenue you can reach profitability, but we don’t know how training costs will change

insights.claim: It is unclear how much of the recent surge in AI usage has a quantifiable ROI that a CFO could defend, which makes it hard to know what price levels buyers will actually sustain for current or future use cases.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Demand-side willingness to pay is unproven at scale; revenue growth from current AI usage may not be as sticky as supply-side capex commitments assume.
insights.quote: it’s unclear how much of the surge in use in the last few months has an ROI (or at least has an ROI that can be quantified to a CFO)

themes.theme: Four variables that decide whether frontier labs capture value

insights.claim: The single biggest driver of future token pricing is how many use cases are willing to pay to run on the most expensive frontier model versus how many are served just as well by smaller, cheap, commoditized models.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Watch the mix shift of enterprise workloads toward smaller/open models as a leading indicator of frontier pricing power eroding.
insights.locator: para 2, 'how many people will pay to be at the top right of the curve'

insights.claim: A second determinant is whether the capability frontier keeps advancing fast enough, and keeps needing enough additional compute, to stay ahead of the downward pricing pressure from efficiency gains and added capacity.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: If frontier progress plateaus while efficiency keeps improving, pricing power collapses faster than capex assumptions currently price in.
insights.quote: does that continue to happen at a rate that keeps it ahead of downward pricing pressure from efficiency and capacity gains?

insights.claim: Whether frontier labs retain pricing power also depends on whether the field consolidates to fewer players (or diverges by specialty) versus staying a mid-single-digit group of labs producing largely interchangeable models on the same science and training data.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: No network effect has yet been identified that lets one frontier lab pull decisively ahead; absent that, expect continued price competition among roughly comparable frontier models.
insights.quote: At the moment, everyone is using mostly the same science and mostly the same training data, and getting mostly the same results, and we don’t yet know of a network effect or any other winner-takes-all effect that would let one company pull ahead

insights.claim: Mark Zuckerberg's and Elon Musk's labs went from having no competitive standing to reappearing on frontier model leaderboards within the last six months, illustrating that the competitive field is still opening up rather than consolidating.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: New, well-capitalized entrants re-entering the frontier race argues against a near-term narrowing to two or three winners, favoring the commodity-infrastructure outcome.
insights.quote: in the last six months, Mark Zuckerberg and Elon Musk jumped from zero back onto the leaderboards
insights.locator: closing section, 'maybe frontier models will become less competitive'

insights.claim: The fourth question is how much of the value from high-end AI use cases is captured by the frontier model itself versus by the tooling, proprietary data, go-to-market, and support layered on top of it by other companies.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: If value accrues to the application layer rather than the model layer, investors should favor companies building workflow/data moats on top of models over the model labs themselves.
insights.quote: the value is in what you build on top - after all, every SaaS company is a ’database wrapper’

insights.claim: Chatbots specifically are a weak interface that is likely to struggle to capture value further up the software stack, in Evans' assessment.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Discounts the bull case that consumer chatbot products (e.g., ChatGPT-style interfaces) alone can become the durable value-capture layer; favor purpose-built product wrappers instead.
insights.quote: I’ve argued at length that I think chatbots are a poor interface that will struggle to capture value up the stack

themes.theme: Historical analogies point to a wide range of outcomes, not a predictable one

insights.claim: The popular comparison of today's AI buildout to the dotcom-era fiber overbuild is weak because fiber construction was fixed-cost (digging holes) and ran far ahead of demand, whereas AI compute is marginal-cost (buying more chips) and currently runs behind demand.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: The fiber-glut bear case for AI capex is structurally mismatched; a better mental model is a capacity-constrained marginal-cost buildout, not a stranded-fixed-asset glut.
insights.quote: fiber construction was mostly fixed cost (digging holes) rather than marginal cost (more equipment), whereas growth in compute needs means you need to buy more compute

insights.claim: Mobile data is a more fruitful analogy for AI tokens than fiber: cellular carriers had marginal-cost capacity, an enormous multi-decade usage surge, and an opaque per-bit pricing unit — yet despite revenue reaching a trillion dollars a year and $200 billion in annual capex, mobile carrier stocks went nowhere because value was captured by companies further up the stack.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Even massive, durable usage growth in a marginal-cost infrastructure business does not guarantee investor returns if value migrates to companies built on top — a cautionary comparison for AI infrastructure/compute investors.
insights.quote: in the last 20 years cellular data traffic has risen by several orders of magnitude, and this has become an enormous industry, with annual revenue of a trillion dollars and capex of $200 billion, but the stocks have gone nowhere, and all the value was captured by other people further up the stack

insights.claim: Semiconductor manufacturing shows that escalating frontier costs can shrink a competitive field down to a near-monopoly (Rock's Law: the cost of a cutting-edge fab doubles roughly every four years, which took the field from dozens of players to essentially just TSMC).
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: If AI training costs follow a similar escalation curve, the number of viable frontier labs could shrink sharply even without a classic network effect — a scenario worth monitoring via training-run cost trends.
insights.quote: Rock's Law pointed out that the cost of a cutting edge fab doubles every four years: over time the frontier of semiconductors become so hard and so expensive that the number of players dropped from dozens to a handful and now really only one, TSMC

insights.claim: Even TSMC, which holds a de facto monopoly on frontier chip manufacturing, captured relatively little of the broader tech economy's value — its net income last year was $53 billion, less than half of Apple's alone.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Monopoly status at the frontier does not automatically translate into outsized value capture relative to companies further up the stack — a check on the bull case that a dominant frontier AI lab would necessarily be the biggest value-capture winner.
insights.quote: while TSMC has a de facto monopoly on the frontier, and nice margins, it doesn't actually capture a large share of value from the broader tech economy: net income last year was $53bn, less than half of Apple alone

insights.claim: Sam Altman has, within the same six-month period, compared OpenAI both to Windows (a high-margin, capital-light monopoly built on network effects) and to electricity utilities (natural monopolies that are low-margin, regulated commodity sellers) — two analogies that point to opposite pricing-power outcomes.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Even the CEO most incentivized to project pricing power is hedging between mutually exclusive outcomes, which is itself a signal that the eventual market structure is genuinely undetermined.
insights.quote: in the last six months Sam Altman has compared OpenAI both to Windows, a high-margin capital-light monopoly based on network effects, and electricity utilities, which are natural monopolies but also low-margin regulated utilities selling a pure commodity

insights.claim: Analogies themselves have no predictive value: the widely-made claim 15 years ago that Android would beat iOS because 'open' Wintel had beaten the 'closed' Mac is cited as a cautionary example of clever people wrongly assuming a past pattern would repeat.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Investors should treat every AI-market analogy (fiber, mobile, TSMC, Windows, utilities, nuclear) as illustrative of a range of possible outcomes, not as a forecast of which one AI will follow.
insights.quote: But none of these have predictive value: analogies don’t have predictive value. You can't prove whether something will have the same outcome as mobile by arguing how much it’s like mobile

themes.theme: Wildcards that could break the default commodity-infrastructure path

insights.claim: China is reportedly considering regulating open-source AI models, and people close to Trump have floated similar ideas in the US, meaning export controls and regulation — not just market dynamics — could reshape which models are available and at what cost.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: A regulatory clampdown on open-source models in either the US or China would remove a key deflationary competitive force on frontier pricing — worth tracking as a tail-risk catalyst for frontier-lab pricing power.
insights.quote: China is reportedly considering regulating open source and some people close to Trump have floated this as well

insights.claim: Since Meta abandoned its Llama open-weight model line, the US currently has no leading open-source frontier model, removing a source of competitive/pricing pressure on closed frontier labs.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: The absence of a credible US open-weight frontier competitor modestly strengthens near-term pricing power for closed labs like OpenAI and Anthropic until a new open contender emerges.
insights.quote: since Meta abandoned Llama the US has no leading open model

insights.claim: Lobbying for AI regulation by labs such as Anthropic and OpenAI can be read as regulatory capture — an attempt by incumbents to shape rules in ways that entrench their position rather than purely address safety concerns.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Investors should weigh frontier labs' public regulatory advocacy as a potential strategic moat-building move, not take safety framing at face value.
insights.quote: Many people see the pleas for regulation from Anthropic and (sometimes) OpenAI as a front for regulatory capture

themes.theme: Base case: drift toward commodity infrastructure

insights.claim: Evans' base case is that as the current supply crunch eases, frontier AI models will drift toward becoming low-margin commodity infrastructure, with most value captured by companies building on top of them, unless some currently invisible factor (new network effect, an execution-driven winner, or regulatory intervention) changes that trajectory.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Default portfolio positioning should favor application-layer and infrastructure-adjacent companies that capture value built on top of models, rather than assuming frontier labs themselves retain durable pricing power.
insights.quote: the current market dynamics point to a future in which, as today’s supply crunch eases, frontier models move towards becoming commodity infrastructure, with all of the value built on top, and for a different outcome, something needs to happen that we don’t see yet

insights.claim: The realistic range of outcomes spans two extremes: a winner-take-most future where a single lab such as Anthropic (or an unknown newcomer) dominates and sets its own terms, versus a highly fragmented future of real-time router auctions allocating tasks across hundreds of low-margin model-farms with benchmark intermediaries taking a fee on each transaction.
insights.speaker: Benedict Evans
insights.so_what: Frame AI-infrastructure investment scenarios as a spectrum rather than a binary bet; position for the commoditized middle-to-fragmented end given the article's stated base case.
insights.quote: there’s a future in which Anthropic (or a company we haven’t heard of yet) wins the whole thing and can set its own terms, and a future in which dozens of routers run real-time auctions to allocate your tasks across hundreds of low-margin model-farms and a benchmark company takes a fee on every single one
